body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
irrlicht_observes: partial

## Reasoning

### Agent supports the primitive (yes)

The scenario's **Primitive exercised** is a shell-escape mechanism — "Claude Code `!cmd`, or analogous." Aider implements the exact same primitive: the in-chat `/run` command runs a shell command locally (no LLM round-trip) and `!` is its documented shorthand alias. Per aider's in-chat command reference, `/run` is "Run a shell command and optionally add the output to the chat (alias: !)". So aider's `!echo hello` is a direct one-to-one analog of Claude Code's `!cmd` shell-escape this scenario is modeled on — not a loose stand-in. (https://aider.chat/docs/usage/commands.html)

### irrlicht observes it — but as a tool call, not as a distinct shell-escape (partial)

The canonical irrlicht primitive is narrower than "can the daemon see anything happen": it is that **the parser handles shell-escape output as something distinct from a formal tool-call/result pair, and still resolves to `ready`**. Against that bar, aider lands at `partial`.

State resolution is correct. The transport is `FilesUnderCWD{Filename: ".aider.chat.history.md", RawLineParser}` (core/adapters/inbound/agents/aider/agent.go:45-51). When the user runs a shell command, aider writes a `> Running <cmd>` blockquote into the markdown history. The parser's `runningRE = ^>\s*Running\s+` matches that line (parser.go:69-70) and routes it through `p.toolCall("Bash")` (parser.go:122-124). A turn was opened by the preceding `#### ` user-prompt line; the synthesized `turn_done` arrives via `IdleFlush` after the 1500ms idle window (parser.go:216-229). Net lifecycle: `ready → working → ready`, with no stuck-in-working state. The user-observable signal the scenario asserts ("state does not stick in `working` after the shell command exits") therefore holds.

What makes this `partial` rather than `yes` is the *shape* of the observation. Aider does NOT keep the shell-escape distinct from a formal tool-call/result pair — it folds it into the very same `tool_result` path as `> Applied edit to <file>` (parser.go:119-124, both call `p.toolCall(...)`). `p.toolCall("Bash")` emits a `tool_result` ParsedEvent carrying a `Bash` ToolUse and a matching ToolResultID (parser.go:231-242), unit-confirmed by `TestParser_ToolCall_RunningShell` (parser_test.go:162-172). This is the opposite of the claudecode reference behavior, where the parser deliberately *skips* the `<bash-input>`/`<bash-stdout>` wrapper events so no synthetic tool call is fabricated. The matrix note captures this precisely: "observability folds into '> Running' tool marker."

Concrete downstream consequence: the scenario's `verify.tool_calls_max: 0` (scenarios.json) is **not satisfiable** for aider — a single Bash tool call is emitted per `/run`/`!` invocation, so any aider recording of this cell would show `tool_calls == 1`, not `0`. That divergence from the claudecode-shaped spec is exactly why the verdict is `partial` and why the irrlicht primitive ("distinct from a tool-call/result pair") is only partially met.

### Capability key

The Primitive maps to existing `capabilities.json` keys, not a dedicated `shell_escape` key (none exists in any adapter). The `/run`/`!` surface is gated by `slash_commands: true`; the observation channel is `tool_calls: true` — aider observes the shell-escape *as* a Bash tool call. Both are `true` for aider.

## Verdict implication

`applicable` = yes (agent_supports yes + irrlicht_observes partial). The cell is recipe-eligible, but any recorded spec must accept `tool_calls >= 1` for aider rather than the claudecode `tool_calls_max: 0`, and assert only the state arc + final `ready`.
caveats: feature-invisible-but-spec-compliant inverse: the shell-escape IS visible, but irrlicht cannot distinguish it from a formal tool call — `> Running <cmd>` is parsed as a Bash `tool_result`, the same path as `> Applied edit`. The canonical primitive ('distinct from a tool-call/result pair') is therefore only partially met even though the ready→working→ready arc is correct.

The scenario's claudecode-shaped verify.tool_calls_max: 0 cannot hold for aider — exactly one Bash tool call is emitted per /run or ! invocation. An aider recipe/spec must assert tool_calls >= 1 and check only the state topology + final ready, not a zero tool-call count.

Working→ready latency is idle-driven (IdleFlush after ~1500ms, gated by the detector's 5s working-refresh poll), so the ready transition trails the shell command's exit by up to several seconds rather than firing on the command's completion line.

sources.note: In-chat commands: '/run — Run a shell command and optionally add the output to the chat (alias: !)'. Confirms aider's !cmd shell-escape is the direct analog of Claude Code's !cmd.

runningRE ^>\s*Running\s+ (L69-70) → toolCall("Bash") (L122-124); toolCall emits a tool_result with a Bash ToolUse (L231-242); turn closes via IdleFlush turn_done (L216-229). Shell-escape folds into the same tool path as > Applied edit (L119) — not kept distinct.
TestParser_ToolCall_RunningShell (L162-172): '> Running echo hello' produces exactly one Bash ToolUse — unit confirmation that aider's shell-escape is observed as a tool call.
Source = FilesUnderCWD{.aider.chat.history.md, RawLineParser} (L45-51) — the markdown transcript that carries the '> Running' line the parser keys on.
No shell_escape key; slash_commands:true gates /run, tool_calls:true is the observation channel. Maps the 'shell-escape mechanism' primitive to existing keys.
